The 4 Largest Bomber Fleets In The US Air Force

A closer look at some of the biggest warbirds in America.

The USAF's four primary bomber platforms—B-52, B-1, B-2, and B-21—represent over 70 years of continuous strategic airpower evolution with significant operational and maintenance cost implications.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

Tropical Cyclone Narelle Crosses Australia

The powerful storm lashed the northern edge of the continent with damaging winds and drenching rain as it made landfall multiple times.

Cyclone Narelle's multiple landfalls across northern Australia may disrupt airport operations and require airlines to reassess regional flight scheduling and maintenance facility access for days.

See NASA →

Boeing 787 Range Boost: FAA Approves 400 Extra Miles For The Dreamliner

Boeing's best widebody is getting an upgrade.

Extended 787 range opens new direct route possibilities between secondary cities, reducing airline reliance on hub-and-spoke models and potentially shifting competitive dynamics.

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5 risk assessment frameworks provide structured methodology for identifying hazards systematically rather than relying on reactive incident analysis alone.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Brussels Airlines BRU-JFK: distinctly Belgian and rather decadent

Brussels Airlines, part of the Lufthansa Group, is an odd duck. Despite operating in a unique market, Africa, and being the main airline serving the de facto political capital of Europe, its long-haul fleet comprises ageing Airbus A330s with a variety of business class cabins. While some of the t...

Legacy fleet and fragmented cabin configurations limit Brussels Airlines' competitive positioning on premium transatlantic routes versus modernized competitors.

See Runway Girl Network →

How Much Does An Airbus A350-900 Cost In 2026?

Despite its high list price, the A350-900's presence on the global stage is massive.

Airlines typically negotiate significant discounts off Airbus's published A350-900 list price, making actual acquisition costs substantially lower than headline figures suggest.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB Team Still En Route To LaGuardia: Delayed By TSA Lines

The NTSB is undertaking a complete walking inspection of the LaGuardia crash site, facing a tremendous amount of debris strewn across the runway and taxiways.

TSA checkpoint delays are now impacting accident investigation response times, highlighting a systemic vulnerability in emergency personnel access protocols.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 24, 2026

The B-2 Spirit Bomber’s Flat Architecture Explained: Radar Evasion Tactics For Pilots

Inside America's tailless, flying wing stealth bomber.

The B-2's flying wing design minimizes radar cross-section by eliminating vertical surfaces that reflect electromagnetic waves, making it the benchmark for modern stealth aircraft architecture.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS transforms aviation safety from post-accident investigation into continuous risk identification, reducing incidents before they occur rather than learning from them.

The 5 Largest US Air Force Bases By Active Aircraft Count In 2026

From trainers to transport jets, explore the 5 largest US Air Force bases by aircraft count in 2026 and how they power global operations.

Concentration of military airlift and training capacity at five bases creates strategic vulnerability and logistics dependencies that shape global force deployment timelines.

Safety Mar 23, 2026

Air Canada Flight Attendant Thrown 100 Meters In Jump Seat During LaGuardia Crash

A flight attendant's miraculous survival after being thrown more than 100 meters from an Air Canada Express plane during a fatal crash at LGA.

The flight attendant's ejection from the aircraft suggests structural failure patterns that warrant immediate review of similar regional turboprop designs for latent vulnerabilities.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 23, 2026

Why Newer Aircraft Aren’t Always Better

The newest jets promise efficiency and luxury, but hidden trade-offs lurk beneath the shiny exterior.

Modern aircraft design trade operational simplicity and maintenance accessibility for efficiency gains, creating hidden lifecycle costs that operators must evaluate beyond initial specifications.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 23, 2026

Space Force adds cyber units to guard rocket launches

‘Cyber defense’ squadrons at Cape Canaveral and Vandenberg reflect growing concern over malicious hacks The post Space Force adds cyber units to guard rocket launches appeared first on SpaceNews.

Military space operations now require dedicated cyber defense infrastructure, signaling that ground-based digital security is as critical as physical launch site protection.

See SpaceNews →

▶20:47
Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Policy & Safety Promotion

This video provides an overview of implementing a safety management system (SMS), specifically aligned with FAA Part 5 regulations. Join us as we explore Blue Skies Charter, a fictional company, and learn about general SMS requirements, safety policy development and safety promotion strategies. This video covers essential components like leadership commitment, gap analysis, safety objectives, emergency response planning and employee training. Whether you're a small charter operator or interested in aviation safety, this video offers valuable insights into building an effective SMS.

Small charter operators can leverage practical SMS implementation frameworks to meet FAA Part 5 requirements without requiring enterprise-scale resources or consultants.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

The Real Reason Why Boeing Is Building The F-47 Stealth Fighter And Not Lockheed

The jet will be built on stealth, speed, and strategy.

Boeing's F-47 development signals a strategic pivot to compete in next-generation fighter contracts where stealth integration and manufacturing capability matter as much as established relationships.

Aerospace Mar 23, 2026

NASA Data Hackathon Inspires Community Action

On Jan. 31, students, library staff, researchers, and community members gathered at the University of Florida’s (UF) Marston Science Library for the Environmental Monitoring through Education, Research, and Geospatial Engagement (EMERGE) NASA Data Hackathon. This initiative empowers libraries, ed...

NASA's data hackathon model demonstrates how open geospatial datasets can mobilize non-traditional partners like libraries to address environmental monitoring challenges with practical applications.

See NASA →

Confirmed: This Is Emirates' Only US Route That's Still Suspended

Eight in ten travelers transit via Dubai from Orlando to other international destinations

Emirates' suspension of its Orlando route represents a strategic shift away from lower-connecting-traffic US gateways despite hub-dependent revenue models.

FAA approves higher takeoff weights for Boeing 787-9 and 787-10

Boeing said on March 23, 2026, that the Federal Aviation Administration has certified increased maximum takeoff weight options… The post FAA approves higher takeoff weights for Boeing 787-9 and 787-10 appeared first on AeroTime.

Higher certified takeoff weights enable 787s to carry increased payload or fuel, directly improving route flexibility and cargo revenue potential for operators.

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Risk Management

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety risk management, which determines the need for, and adequacy of, new or revised risk controls based on the assessment of acceptable risk.

Small operators must now systematically evaluate risk controls rather than rely on ad-hoc safety practices, fundamentally changing how they approach operational compliance.

Two of the three encounters took place over the weekend.

Rising attacks on law enforcement aircraft signal a critical safety threat requiring urgent coordination between aviation operations and ground security protocols.

Aerospace Mar 23, 2026

Amazon Leo targets faster deployment cadence as deadline pressure mounts

Amazon vowed to double the annual launch rate for its low Earth orbit broadband constellation to more than 20 missions, hinging largely on rockets yet to prove themselves at scale. The post Amazon Leo targets faster deployment cadence as deadline pressure mounts appeared first on SpaceNews.

Amazon's LEO constellation success depends on unproven launch vehicles reaching operational reliability, creating significant execution risk against regulatory licensing deadlines.

ICE deployment to checkpoints signals escalating operational strain beyond TSA's core capacity, potentially creating friction between security missions and passenger throughput.

Military Mar 23, 2026

Colombian military C-130 crashes after takeoff with 125 aboard

A Colombian Air Force Lockheed Martin C-130 Hercules with 125 people on board crashed shortly after takeoff on… The post Colombian military C-130 crashes after takeoff with 125 aboard appeared first on AeroTime.

Military transport operations face critical risk during initial climb phases when aircraft weight and configuration limit recovery options from engine or system failures.

Industry Mar 24, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization through integrated technology adoption across airlines, airports, and flight management offers a more achievable near-term pathway to sustainability than waiting for fleet-wide aircraft replacement.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 23, 2026

Science Through Shadows: How Astronomical Alignments Reveal the Universe

When one celestial object passes in front of another, it can cast a shadow that travels across space – and sometimes across Earth. These moments of alignment, known as eclipses, occultations, and transits, allow scientists to study distant objects in remarkable ways. By observing how light change...

Astronomical alignment science provides precise validation methods for aircraft navigation and timing systems that depend on celestial reference points.

See NASA →

ICE Agents Assigned To Airports to Alleviate TSA Staffing Strain

ICE and Homeland Security Investigations personnel sent to 14 airports amid TSA absenteeism and resignations during partial shutdown.

ICE deployment signals DHS is reallocating enforcement resources to maintain airport security operations, revealing structural vulnerabilities in TSA's staffing resilience during government disruptions.

Radiate Engineering & Design AG, based in Zurich, introduces FlyBlocks, a production-ready structuralairframe platform for

FlyBlocks modularizes VTOL airframe design to reduce development cycles and manufacturing complexity for commercial long-range drone operators.

Parsons unveils new satellite antenna aimed at military market

Hybrid antenna developed by Parsons and Raven Defense combines a six-meter parabolic dish with an electronically steered phased-array feed The post Parsons unveils new satellite antenna aimed at military market appeared first on SpaceNews.

The hybrid antenna design enables military operators to achieve both high-gain coverage and electronic beam steering in a single platform, reducing the need for separate ground station equipment.

Military Mar 23, 2026

Remembering the Green Ramp Disaster

Tragedy struck on March 23, 1994, at Pope Air Force Base, North Carolina, on an aircraft parking ramp known as ‘Green Ramp.’ A number of paratroopers were lost and several injured after flames and debris rained down on their training and assembly area after a mid-air collision between a fighter j...

The Green Ramp Disaster killed 24 paratroopers in 1994, making it one of the deadliest peacetime accidents in U.S. military aviation history and a pivotal case study for aircraft separation protocols.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 23, 2026

See NASA’s GUARDIAN Catch a Tsunami

A new data visualization illustrates how an experimental NASA technology can provide extra lead time to communities in the path of a tsunami. Called GUARDIAN (GNSS Upper Atmospheric Real-time Disaster Information and Alert Network), the software detects slight distortions in satellite navigation ...

GUARDIAN's ability to detect ionospheric disturbances from satellites could provide minutes of advance warning before traditional tsunami detection systems, creating critical escape time for coastal populations.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 23, 2026

Sovereign demand and institutional capital reshape space economy

Large infrastructure funds are “carefully looking” into entering the space sector, Seraphim Space CEO Mark Boggett said March 23, giving early-stage investors more confidence to back ambitious startups that may later need billions of dollars to scale. The post Sovereign demand and institutional c...

Institutional capital influx into space ventures signals market maturation, enabling early-stage companies to scale toward commercialization without immediate profitability pressure.

See SpaceNews →
